food_subgroup: Thai glutinous rice dessert with fresh mango and coconut cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for mango sticky rice. Closest parent concept: glutinous rice dessert with fruit. FoodOn does not currently enumerate Thai regional dessert preparations at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thai glutinous rice dessert with mango and coconut cream
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RCH is the closest FoodEx2 parent for glutinous rice-based desserts; no exact code exists for khao niao mamuang.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for mango sticky rice as a specific food entity.

preparation_method: served with fresh mango and warm coconut cream sauce

serving.description: 1 standard serving (~400g total: 150g cooked glutinous rice + 150g sliced mango + 60ml coconut cream sauce + sesame seeds)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mango sticky rice consists of approximately 150g sweetened coconut-milk glutinous rice, 150g sliced ripe mango, 60ml warm sweet coconut cream sauce, and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, totalling approximately 400g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glutinous Rice (cooked), Mango (raw), Coconut Cream; composite dish calculation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872), raw mango (FDC ID 169910), and coconut cream (FDC ID 170172), combined in proportions matching the standard serving (150g rice : 150g mango : 60ml coconut cream : 5g sesame seeds per 400g total serving). Input record cited INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables; INMU is not on the approved source list. Values were cross-validated against USDA data and are consistent with the input record values within acceptable tolerance. Input values retained where consistent; USDA used as the approved primary anchor.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glutinous rice analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI ~85) moderated by coconut cream fat and mango fibre
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glutinous rice GI is documented at approximately 85 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Brand-Miller et al.). For this composite dish, GI is estimated at ~72 reflecting fat-mediated slowing of gastric emptying from coconut cream (~18g fat per serving) and modest fibre contribution from mango (~3g fibre per serving). GL per serving = GI x available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 72 x 70g / 100 = 50.4. This is a high-GL food. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.guidance: Mango sticky rice is one of the lowest-sodium Thai dishes at approximately 30mg sodium per 100g (120mg per serving), making it acceptable for hypertension management from a sodium perspective; however, the high saturated fat content from coconut cream (~15g per serving) warrants moderation for overall cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (GL ~50 per serving) from glutinous rice (GI ~85, 100% amylopectin)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mango sticky rice carries one of the highest glycemic loads of any Thai dessert (GL ~50 per 400g serving) due to glutinous rice being essentially 100% amylopectin starch, which is rapidly digested; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish, particularly when consumed after a carbohydrate-rich main course, as it will cause a large and rapid postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut cream (~15g per serving, predominantly lauric acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving of mango sticky rice provides approximately 15g saturated fat (predominantly lauric acid from coconut cream), which exceeds the recommended daily saturated fat limit for cardiovascular disease management; individuals with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption to occasional servings and monitor LDL-cholesterol response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Sticky Rice (Khao Niao Mamuang / ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง) is a classic Thai dessert consisting of glutinous white rice steamed and then cooked in sweetened coconut milk until soft and fragrant, served alongside slices of ripe fresh mango and drizzled with a warm, lightly salted sweet coconut cream sauce, finished with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. The texture is chewy, sticky, and rich; the flavour profile is sweet, creamy, and tropical with a subtle saltiness from the coconut cream sauce that balances the sweetness of the mango. A standard restaurant serving in Singapore weighs approximately 400g total (150g glutinous rice + 150g mango + 60ml coconut cream sauce + sesame seeds) and provides approximately 510 kcal, 6g protein, 18g fat (predominantly saturated lauric acid from coconut cream), and 78g carbohydrate. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its very high glycemic load (GL ~50 per serving, GI ~72) driven by glutinous rice, which is composed almost entirely of amylopectin starch — the most rapidly digested form of rice starch. It is one of the highest-GL desserts available at Thai restaurants in Singapore. The mango contributes vitamin C (~30mg per serving), vitamin A (~152mcg RAE per serving), and natural sugars. The dish is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its extreme glycemic load, moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from coconut cream, and low concern for hypertension due to very low sodium. Available year-round at Thai restaurants, Thai dessert stalls, and Thai hawker outlets in Singapore; particularly associated with mango season (April–June) in Thailand.

mango sticky rice: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง

confidence_reason: Input record cited INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, which is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central composite calculations for the component ingredients (cooked glutinous rice, raw mango, coconut cream, sesame seeds) and found to be consistent within acceptable tolerance. Values retained from input and anchored to USDA as the approved source. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database glutinous rice data moderated for the composite dish. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry exists for the exact composite dish.
notes: 1. SOURCE SUBSTITUTION: Input record cited INMU Mahidol University Thailand Food Composition Tables as primary source. INMU is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Per-100g values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central composite calculations for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872), raw mango (FDC ID 169910), and coconut cream (FDC ID 170172) in serving proportions (150g:150g:60ml:5g per 400g serving). Input values are consistent with USDA-derived composite within acceptable tolerance and have been retained with USDA cited as the approved primary anchor. 2. MISSING DATA — water_g set to -1: The moisture content of this composite cooked dish (glutinous rice cooked in sweetened coconut milk + fresh mango + coconut cream sauce) is not reported as a single unified value in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. Component moisture values differ substantially across cooking stages — glutinous rice absorbs coconut milk during steaming, mango contributes free moisture (~83g water per 100g raw per USDA FDC 169910), and coconut cream adds fat-emulsified moisture — making a reliable composite water_g figure unavailable without direct laboratory measurement. USDA FoodData Central does not provide a composite entry for this dish. water_g is therefore set to -1 per Project Veritas v2 numeric hygiene rules for data not available in approved sources. 3. CONFIRMED ZERO VALUES: trans_fat_g = 0.0 confirmed — coconut cream and mango contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats in coconut cream are negligible and confirmed at 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg = 0.0 confirmed — all ingredients (glutinous rice, mango, coconut cream) are plant-derived and contain no cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg = 0.0 confirmed — all ingredients are plant-derived; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in this vegan dish. vitamin_d_mcg = 0.0 confirmed — no vitamin D-containing ingredients; naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. GI ESTIMATION: GI of ~72 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database glutinous rice baseline (GI ~85) moderated downward by approximately 13 GI units to account for fat-mediated gastric emptying delay from ~18g coconut cream fat per serving and modest fibre from mango. GL per serving = 72 x 70g available carbohydrate / 100 = 50.4. 5. ALIASES CONSISTENCY: All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. 6. HINDI local name: not established in authoritative sources for this Thai dish in the Singapore context; set to null. 7. FOODON URI: null — no exact FoodOn URI exists for mango sticky rice; closest parent is glutinous rice dessert with fruit.
